ground_truth.description: lower_expr_iterative() claims to be an iterative replacement for the recursive lower_expr(), but it falls back to recursive calls for most complex cases (binary_expression calls self.lower_binary_expr which is recursive, the _ arm calls self.lower_expr which is fully recursive). This defeats the purpose of the iterative approach and provides no stack overflow protection for the cases that actually need it.

For binary_expression, the code pushes children onto the stack AND calls self.lower_binary_expr(node) which processes the same children recursively. This double-processes child nodes — once via the recursive call and again when they're popped from the stack later, producing duplicate/corrupted results.

results.into_iter().last().flatten() returns the last expression pushed, but the stack is LIFO — the root node is processed first (popped first from the initial push) and its result is pushed first into results. For a tree with children, the last result will be from the deepest leaf, not the root expression. The function returns the wrong AST node.

lower_expr_iterative() duplicates the dispatch logic from lower_expr() (matching on node.kind()) without reusing it. Any new expression types added to lower_expr() would need to be added to both functions.
